speaker: interviewer
text: 30th of July, 1996, and we we're in Sydney, Australia. Camera's ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: Harold, we just got to the end of the war, and you went back to London, and you started a new career in selling. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was life like in London after the war? How was life for your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was difficult. Getting used to London-- getting used to life in London. See, being in the Army is a different life, altogether. You're restricted, but it's different. Now going back to London, you're on your own.

speaker: interviewer
text: What made you decide to come to Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a shop in London. My wife helped. We had a little dress shop. And my wife, she's suffered with, her fingers, of bad circulation. Two fingers went white. Went to the doctor-- the local doctor. He said, you have to go to a warmer country. Warmer climate. Your fingers-- your circulation is bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, where could we go? We don't know. So we let it slide a bit. A customer came into the shop, and she had a badge on her. A young girl. Badge-- what do you call it? Animal-- Australian animal. Kangaroo.

speaker: interviewee
text: Kangaroo, something like that. And we said, what's that? She said, we're from Australia. She said, why don't you come out to Australia? I said, how should we start? There's a scheme on, she said. $10, cost you each. And the boy, $5. For $25 you come to Australia. But you have to have somebody to sponsor you. Just can't go out there. I had nobody to sponsor me. I went to the Australia House in London, looked up the newspapers, see what jobs that are. Like the Australian newspapers, what job's available. I wrote to some places. Most of them didn't answer.

speaker: interviewer
text: One man did answer called Niglious. You know him? He's dead now. He answered the application. He says, I can't sponsor you, but if you come out to Australia, we'll give you a job. That's good. That was a fur company, wasn't it? It was a big-- that was a big fur company in Sidney.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, his own, [INAUDIBLE]. But then that was good enough for me. I wrote to the synagogue. And they sent-- put my letter to the-- the welfare society. That British ex-soldiers may need sponsoring.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went through the channels, and they decided to sponsor me. And me accommodation. They had a house in Northshore, don't you know it?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That's [INAUDIBLE]. And they could keep me-- keep us for-- until we find a job, or somewhere to live. So we decided to come out.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what year was it when you came to Australia?

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old was Harold at that time? Your son.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he'd left school. He'd finished school.

speaker: interviewee
text: He left school. He found a job. Precious metals-- working in the office.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said, Harold, when we come out to Australia, you've got to find a job. I can't help you at all, because I don't know anything about Australia. I said, what do you want to be?

speaker: interviewee
text: He doesn't want to be a solicitor. You've got to learn Latin. He said, I don't want to. He wanted to do broadcasting. I said find yourself a job. Same as me. I'm going to find myself a job, as well. We came to Australia, Sidney. He was [INAUDIBLE] for jobs. He went to 2GB, I think it was.

speaker: interviewee
text: Radio-- radio, yes. Do anything in broadcasting. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They had nothing for him. They said-- this is-- during the week. Come in on Monday, we'll find you a job. This is about Wednesday. About five or six days later. Wasn't good enough for Herald. Straight away he went to-- not 2GB. 2SM-- no, the other one.

speaker: interviewee
text: 2UE, yes. 2UE. We Can you find him a job? Yes, we'll take you on straight away. Of course, already, he'd passed the exam, and he was a bright boy.

speaker: interviewee
text: They gave him a job, like, running errands. What are they called? At the desk. Whenever anybody would need boys to run errands or help, they'd go to the desk. They'd give the boys first opportunity. And he was able to build a career in--

speaker: interviewee
text: He had one job, running around. Then I think-- he went to 2GB after that. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They had a job-- used to help making up productions, and so on. Fitting up the studio for black productions, and so on. When they had plays-- plays on, yes. Then one opportunity came his way, something happened. One of the outsiders who'd-- who got the news was away, or not available. They put-- a speaker--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, what do you call it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Microphone. They said, Harold, go out and get a story. He went.

speaker: interviewee
text: He became a journalist. He came back with a good story. Another good story he was sent on, the-- the moorship that ran down a boat-- I forget the name. He was turned out to do the story. And he got a story.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they put him on broadcasting. Getting the story, not actually speaking on the radio. Getting the story, writing up, that was his job. And what about your job? What did you do in Australia? Did you sty in the fur business?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there was no fur business. No, it's finished. Who could afford luxury furs? No.

speaker: interviewee
text: I answered adverts, selling. I got one, a British company wanted a salesman, outdoor. I answered. You know which one it was? [? Costitna. ?] I took it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you worked for them until you retired.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. And did your son get married and have children? Do you have grandchildren? Would you like to say names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We've got three grandchildren, Mark, Matthew, and Elissa, the girl. Now Mark-- should I speak about?

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, just go back a little way. Can you tell me about your wife, Esther? What did she do in Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: She didn't work. My work had to keep her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And she's here in the Montefiore Home with you, too?

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And you belong to the Association of Jewish Ex-servicemen?

speaker: interviewer
text: NAJEX. Do you ever talk to the other veterans about--

speaker: interviewer
text: What do-- what do they say? Did they have similar-- similar experiences to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, most were Australian. Yes. Most of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were not in-- they weren't in Europe. They were in Japan?

speaker: interviewer
text: In the Pacific. All right. Do you ever talk to your son or your grandchildren about your wartime experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I used to. They know it all.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because when I told Harold that I was going to speak here, he knew all about it, yes. Because he's heard it before.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you talked to anybody else about it, though? Have you ever recorded it before? No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: And have you ever been back to Europe? To-- Oh, yes, three times.

speaker: interviewee
text: At one time I organized a tour around the world. I said, do it now, while we're still young. When we get old, we can't walk, it's no good.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went to Hong Kong, then on to Europe, London. I had around the world tickets. For a plane. You could hop off whatever you want to. But you've never been back to Germany, or to those places where--

speaker: interviewer
text: No. How do you feel when you meet Germans? When you talk to German people?

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't talk about your experiences.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Because I spoke to the German people. I was friendly with them. It's no good, well, saying, it's your fault. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. Have you ever been to Israel? Have you been to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was too much out of the way.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had an orthodox upbringing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you still orthodox? Are you still--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. My wife is very orthodox. Yes. She came from a very orthodox family.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. So you didn't-- you didn't lose your faith after what you saw--

speaker: interviewer
text: --in Germany? All right. And is there anything else you'd like to say just now, to your grandchildren, or to the younger generation? Would you like to--

speaker: interviewee
text: You don't see much of them now. Matthew-- Mark is in England, living there, don't see him at all. Matthew just came back from Japan. He's found a job here. He's going to get settled down soon.

speaker: interviewee
text: The girl, she's living with her mother. Because you know Harold got divorced.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there-- is there anything else you'd like to say about-- about your story? About your life or your time in the war? Have we covered it, most of the story?

speaker: interviewee
text: More or less. Important parts, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Thank you, Joseph, for telling your story. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you say what this is, Joseph?

speaker: interviewer
text: It's the document from the synagogue. From the United Synagogue in 1934.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's a document that says your parents were married.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right, that's right. Proof that they were married.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a copy of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you needed that when you were getting married.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was this one, Joseph? That's when I was 17.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My-- going to a wedding. I had to look good.

speaker: interviewee
text: About that time, yes. And I had hair then.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was-- what was her maiden name? Rosenblatt. Esther Rosenblatt. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: The wedding photograph of Esther and Joe, March the 18th, 1934.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's Esther, on her own, March, the 18th, 1934. Wedding photograph.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a holiday photograph, Esther and Harold. Harold was only about two, then.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that just before the war started?

speaker: interviewee
text: Photograph of Esther and Harold, taken during the war, yes. Well, they took it at Cambridge, when they were evacuated.

speaker: interviewee
text: Taken at the end of the war. You see the uniform I'm wearing. We were allowed to wear a collar and tie then. Before the war, during the war, we weren't allowed. Had to be buttoned up to the top.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was taken in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Germany. All right. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go. Esther at a ball, dressed up-- well, going to the ball.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Sydney, yes. She was always interested in charities. She ran a shop in the, city selling toys. Charity shop.

speaker: interviewee
text: She's-- she's a JP, yes. She's interested in oil paintings. Only-- everything for higher grade.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's Esther in one of the charity shops that she ran for charity in the city. And the lady that's next to her is Sonia McMahon, who was the wife of the Prime Minister of Australia. Sonia.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's Esther with our first grandchild, Mark. Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's Esther going to the ball. Dressed up in party clothes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's at your house, is it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's Esther and Joe at [INAUDIBLE], Surfer's Paradise. You can see by the hat she's wearing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just at holiday at Surfer's Paradise, having our evening meal.

speaker: interviewer
text: Looks like you're having a good time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Esther holding our dog in our garden. A dog we were very proud of.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was that, Joe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Esther in the Montefiore Home. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. This is-- this is where you both live now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yeah. I wasn't living here then. I was just a volunteer.

speaker: interviewer
text: She's been here for quite some time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Esther in-- and myself as a volunteer in the Montefiore Home. She's in the nursing wing. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: These are four medals given to me at the end of the war. I use them now for [INAUDIBLE] Day, or the Jewish march at the war memorial. As a matter of fact, at the last war memorial one, I laid the wreath of behalf of the Montefiore Home. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you say, Joseph, what the medals are? What they represent?

speaker: interviewer
text: That's the second from the right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. So one is from the civil defense, and three--

speaker: interviewee
text: Three is for Army, yes. For service during the war. In 1939, '44, and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Thank you very much. Thank you for telling your story.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, that's the end of tape three, and the interview with Joseph Abrahams. It's the 30th of July, 1996, and we're in Sydney, Australia. And that's also the end of the whole interview.
